tweets.text:
if your base port secured the whole trade it doesnt really reduce the risk. if meta drops hard it means the rest of your port is likely dropping hard and theres a broad sell off which would be the same time you'd have to come up with the 275k if you get assigned

your upside is capped a 64k no matter what

lets say a broad market sell off happens, meta falls to 300. you are still obligated to pay 550 - premium so 421. and then you have a paper loss of 60,500 after keeping the premium

its not really as risk free as you make it sound in this sense. long dated = more variables

tweets.text:
I wanted to understand why Solana spot volume has been growing, and honestly, the answer was not what I expected.

The obvious assumption is memecoins, because that’s been the main Solana narrative for idk how long now? Like 4 years?

But anyways, the bigger story here is that a lot of the recent volume is actually coming from tokenized assets.

Tokenized asset spot volume on Solana grew from ~$2.69B in Q1 to ~$5.7B in Q2, which was a new quarterly ATH.

And @Solana captured 97% of cumulative onchain tokenized equities spot volume in May, while its broader RWA ecosystem hit $2.8B+ and crossed 230K onchain RWA holders.

On top of that, June made it even clearer.

During the week of June 15–21, Solana processed ~95% of global tokenized stock volume, and daily tokenized equity trading later hit a $644M record. Funny enough, at one point, tokenized equities even passed memecoins as a share of Solana spot volume.

This is probably the most refreshing Solana volume story in a while IMO because there’s actual retail-style trading happening around assets people already understand, like stocks and ETFs, but on faster crypto rails.

IMO, memecoins aren’t going away because they’re still a huge part of Solana.

But I think the more interesting shift is that Solana is starting to look more like a general-purpose spot market for crypto, RWAs, stablecoins, and tokenized equities.

Also, I noticed some Solana prediction markets have been popping up lately, so I might do a post on that next and see how that impacts this.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: JUST IN: Tokenized asset spot volume grew from $2.69B in Q1 to $5.7B in Q2 on Solana https://t.co/Q1WRbPKexD
tweets.full_text:
I wanted to understand why Solana spot volume has been growing, and honestly, the answer was not what I expected.

The obvious assumption is memecoins, because that’s been the main Solana narrative for idk how long now? Like 4 years?

But anyways, the bigger story here is that a lot of the recent volume is actually coming from tokenized assets.

Tokenized asset spot volume on Solana grew from ~$2.69B in Q1 to ~$5.7B in Q2, which was a new quarterly ATH.

And @Solana captured 97% of cumulative onchain tokenized equities spot volume in May, while its broader RWA ecosystem hit $2.8B+ and crossed 230K onchain RWA holders.

On top of that, June made it even clearer.

During the week of June 15–21, Solana processed ~95% of global tokenized stock volume, and daily tokenized equity trading later hit a $644M record. Funny enough, at one point, tokenized equities even passed memecoins as a share of Solana spot volume.

This is probably the most refreshing Solana volume story in a while IMO because there’s actual retail-style trading happening around assets people already understand, like stocks and ETFs, but on faster crypto rails.

IMO, memecoins aren’t going away because they’re still a huge part of Solana.

But I think the more interesting shift is that Solana is starting to look more like a general-purpose spot market for crypto, RWAs, stablecoins, and tokenized equities.

Also, I noticed some Solana prediction markets have been popping up lately, so I might do a post on that next and see how that impacts this.

thread_tweets.text:
amigo, this is longer than my article

in asia, the best presentations were when i was up close with the community, walking through things step-by-step.  (see attached video)

covered bayc, otherside, apecoin - the apecoin content in particular is covered above 

a dialogue more than a lecture. i leave room for myself to be wrong about things and open to changing my pov. but if i'm right, it's a more potent format to build conviction in an audience.

no apechurch wasn't a part of that presentation. made that decision 2 days ago.  ruminated on it while flying back from asia as apes in shanghai expressed potential in the platform.  dug deeper into the app and saw that it was actually the best performing app in its class on any chain.  a 4M house placement seemed like an obvious move to make.  protocols should double down on winners.

apecoin is still the coin of the realm on apechain and otherside.  my argument here is that it's oversold.  there's still a case to be made for the upside beyond the HTF support level (part ii)

think longing bayc/mayc is perfectly fine.  but looking at the ratio, there's a 20:1 preference toward the nft vs the fungible.  like a good car engine, all the parts need to be a bit more balanced for the system to be at its best.  i think 20:1 is too lopsided.  better if we don't have an achilles heel in our eco.

yes there are dedicated teams across these 3 efforts.  but at times they can also become very interrelated so cross-functional roles across multiple areas are sometimes a better fit.

tweets.text:
what is it about data centers that offends you? 

it's a quiet building that performs computation. it brings in massive amounts of revenue that can be used to offset property taxes. it doesn't have to be anywhere near residential areas. it's no more aesthetically offensive than a warehouse. it doesn't consume meaningful water; it does consume power - but are you against florida producing sufficient power to be an industrial powerhouse?

tweets.text:
What can we expect from Myriad #Uranium's $M.CN drill program at Copper Mountain?

At the very least, confirmation of historic results. Myriad exceeded them in its maiden program.

At Lucky Cliff, a historic hole returned 355 ft of 0.027% eU₃O₈.

But the real upside may lie elsewhere 👇

Recent geophysics outside the historic 655 Mlbs uranium endowment area suggests some of the best ground may have been completely missed.

And here's the wildcard:

Historic drilling rarely went below 600 ft. Myriad has already proven deeper mineralization exists.

Could Copper Mountain's huge uranium system be connected at depth? Could deeper, Athabasca-style mineralization exist?

A deeper, high-grade hit could be a game changer.

And even without it, Copper Mountain may already be one of the largest undeveloped uranium opportunities in the U.S. 🎯

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own research.

The first four holes could get the program off to a
tweets.full_text:
What can we expect from Myriad #Uranium's $M.CN drill program at Copper Mountain?

At the very least, confirmation of historic results. Myriad exceeded them in its maiden program.

At Lucky Cliff, a historic hole returned 355 ft of 0.027% eU₃O₈.

But the real upside may lie elsewhere 👇

Recent geophysics outside the historic 655 Mlbs uranium endowment area suggests some of the best ground may have been completely missed.

And here's the wildcard:

Historic drilling rarely went below 600 ft. Myriad has already proven deeper mineralization exists.

Could Copper Mountain's huge uranium system be connected at depth? Could deeper, Athabasca-style mineralization exist?

A deeper, high-grade hit could be a game changer.

And even without it, Copper Mountain may already be one of the largest undeveloped uranium opportunities in the U.S. 🎯

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own research.

tweets.text:
Well said
“Nvidia became Nvidia not because it had great quarters but rather became Nvidia because the market eventually recognized that the demand driver underneath the business, AI compute was not cyclical.

Memory in 2026 is hitting that same inflection point, every model trained, every inference call made and every agentic workflow running in the background is multiplying memory demand exponentially.

The market will eventually stop asking whether this is the peak and start asking why it took so long to recognize what was right in front of it.”
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Memory stocks are becoming the Nvidia of the AI era and the market has no idea how to price them for it (Save this).

Samsung's operating profit went from 4.7 trillion won in Q2 2025 to an expected 89.4 trillion won in Q2 2026, an 18 fold increase in a single year, with a 52% https://t.co/6N5tADryNq

tweets.text:
I’m really excited about what’s going on with Digital Ray, which is my digital twin. I'm creating this so that everyone can have as much contact and as many conversations with me as they want—unlimited amounts, in the same sort of way that I do it. Eventually, it’s going to be a lot smarter than I am.

This is all part of the evolutionary process. It’s still a bit experimental, but it really is something. If you want to have unlimited conversations with me, go online and try it.

I also highly recommend taking the Principles You personality test. It’s online and only takes about 20 or 30 minutes. It will tell you all about your characteristics and inclinations.  Integrating your assessment with Digital Ray allows for highly personalized guidance in Coach Mode, tailored to your unique needs across life and work.

You can find a link here: https://t.co/daCo3GY70r

#DigitalRay #ArtificialIntelligence #PrinciplesYou #RayDalio

tweets.text:
Everyone has an opinion on why the stock market does what it does. 

Earnings. The economy. Who's in the White House. The Fed chair's mood that morning.

Almost all of it is noise.

This chart is 15 years of the Nasdaq 100 plotted against total global liquidity. The correlation is 97%.

When I first saw this I was stunned. A fit this tight meant something much deeper was going on underneath. So I started pulling the thread.

That thread became the Everything Code, the framework I've been building in GMI since 2023. It’s the single most important thing I can teach anyone about how the modern economy actually works.

tweets.text:
I remember when at my previous company Doctrine we rewrote our frontend from AngularJS 1.7 to React. It was a huge project and a big decision. It took roughly six months and a team of talented software engineers. 

That’s why the Bun rewrite is so interesting to me. Bun was ~535k lines of Zig. The rewrite produced more than 1M lines of Rust diff! But... it took only 11 days. 

At peak they had around 64 agents running in parallel, across 4 worktrees, with 16 agents per workflow. It generated 6,500+ commits (wow!)

It was also a lot of work because they didn't ask "plz Code go from Zig to Rust" but they created a porting guide, a flow to analyze errors, separate adversarial agents to review diff to find mistakes etc. The human job was not to write the code but more designing and supervising the process aka "managing agents."

IMO it's a big deal. The world is full of important software stuck on old frameworks, old languages, old architectures and old security models because rewriting is too expensive and too risky. 

If this methodology becomes reliable, we can move software to safer languages, better runtimes, cleaner architectures and more secure defaults much faster. 

So much software run legacy COBOL and soon the transition to Rust will be easy.
tweets.full_text:
I remember when at my previous company Doctrine we rewrote our frontend from AngularJS 1.7 to React. It was a huge project and a big decision. It took roughly six months and a team of talented software engineers. 

That’s why the Bun rewrite is so interesting to me. Bun was ~535k lines of Zig. The rewrite produced more than 1M lines of Rust diff! But... it took only 11 days. 

At peak they had around 64 agents running in parallel, across 4 worktrees, with 16 agents per workflow. It generated 6,500+ commits (wow!)

It was also a lot of work because they didn't ask "plz Code go from Zig to Rust" but they created a porting guide, a flow to analyze errors, separate adversarial agents to review diff to find mistakes etc. The human job was not to write the code but more designing and supervising the process aka "managing agents."

IMO it's a big deal. The world is full of important software stuck on old frameworks, old languages, old architectures and old security models because rewriting is too expensive and too risky. 

If this methodology becomes reliable, we can move software to safer languages, better runtimes, cleaner architectures and more secure defaults much faster. 

So much software run legacy COBOL and soon the transition to Rust will be easy.

tweets.text:
Microsoft just released TypeScript 7! It’s much faster.  Often 8x to 12x faster on full builds like VS Code went from 125s to ~10s (11x faster!)

There’s a real world where Python remains the language of ML with PyTorch, JAX etc, but TypeScript becomes the language of AI.

Most AI apps are not training models. They’re calling APIs, orchestrating tools, streaming tokens, managing state, auth, billing, workflows, agents, eval dashboards, and UX. Most companies are using TypeScript for that.

That world is getting much more TypeScript-shaped. Basically, Python trains the models and TypeScript ships the product.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Huge milestone for our team today: TypeScript 7 is now generally available--a native port that runs 10x faster. @typescript https://t.co/3DtJJqEZAl
tweets.full_text:
Microsoft just released TypeScript 7! It’s much faster.  Often 8x to 12x faster on full builds like VS Code went from 125s to ~10s (11x faster!)

There’s a real world where Python remains the language of ML with PyTorch, JAX etc, but TypeScript becomes the language of AI.

Most AI apps are not training models. They’re calling APIs, orchestrating tools, streaming tokens, managing state, auth, billing, workflows, agents, eval dashboards, and UX. Most companies are using TypeScript for that.

That world is getting much more TypeScript-shaped. Basically, Python trains the models and TypeScript ships the product.
